readings.title: First reading
readings.text:
The Promise of the Holy Spirit
In the first book,
O Theoph′ilus, I have dealt with all that Jesus began to do and teach,
until the day when he was taken up, after he had given commandment through the Holy Spirit to the apostles whom he had chosen.
To them he presented himself alive after his passion by many proofs, appearing to them during forty days, and speaking of the kingdom of God.
And while staying
with them he charged them not to depart from Jerusalem, but to wait for the promise of the Father, which, he said, “you heard from me,
for John baptized with water, but before many days you shall be baptized with the Holy Spirit.”
The Ascension of Jesus
So when they had come together, they asked him, “Lord, will you at this time restore the kingdom to Israel?”
He said to them, “It is not for you to know times or seasons which the Father has fixed by his own authority.
But you shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samar′ia and to the end of the earth.”
And when he had said this, as they were looking on, he was lifted up, and a cloud took him out of their sight.
And while they were gazing into heaven as he went, behold, two men stood by them in white robes,
and said, “Men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into heaven? This Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven.”
Read full chapter

exegesis.summary: Luke opens the Acts of the Apostles by recalling the forty days of the Risen Christ's appearances and recounting his Ascension from the Mount of Olives. Jesus charges the apostles to wait in Jerusalem for the promised Holy Spirit, who will empower them to be his witnesses to the ends of the earth. As he is taken up in a cloud, two angels promise that he will return in the same way he departed.

patristicQuotes.quote: Why did not the Spirit come while Christ was present, or immediately on His departure? Because it was fitting that they should become desirous of grace, and then at length receive it. It was necessary that our nature should appear in Heaven, and the covenants be completed, and that then the Spirit should come, and pure joys be experienced.

There are diversities of graces. One operation He breathed into them there, another He promised here. For there the grace of remitting sins was given, which is breathed into them by Christ, that you may believe the Holy Spirit to be of Christ, to be from God. But Luke describes the pouring forth of the grace of speaking with tongues.
He promised the Holy Spirit to the Apostles before the Ascension; He gave it to them in fiery tongues, after the Ascension.

cccReferences.title: Promise of the Paraclete after glorification
Spirit sent by Father and Son

exegesis.thematicConnection: This reading narrates the very event today's Solemnity celebrates, framing the Ascension as both an ending and a beginning: the end of Christ's visible earthly mission and the beginning of the Church's apostolic mission — the same mission Matthew's Gospel will spell out in the Great Commission.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
For the
, the Most High, is terrible,
a great king over all the earth.
He subdued peoples under us,
and nations under our feet.
Read full chapter

exegesis.summary: Psalm 47 is one of the great enthronement psalms, sung as God ascends his holy throne amid shouts of joy and the blast of the trumpet. The Church places it on the lips of the faithful today as Christ, the eternal King, ascends to take his place at the Father's right hand. The Lord most high reigns over all the nations.

patristicQuotes.quote: Observe how He both ascends with His Apostles and descends to the multitude; for how could the multitude see Christ but in a lowly place. It follows him not to the lofty places, it ascends not the heights.

cccReferences.title: Royal freedom of Christ shared with disciples

exegesis.thematicConnection: The Psalm sings what the first reading narrates and what the second reading proclaims theologically: God mounts his throne amid shouts of joy. Christ's Ascension is his enthronement as King over every nation — the very nations the Gospel will send the apostles to disciple.

readings.title: Second reading
readings.text:
that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give you a spirit of wisdom and of revelation in the knowledge of him,
having the eyes of your hearts enlightened, that you may know what is the hope to which he has called you, what are the riches of his glorious inheritance in the saints,
and what is the immeasurable greatness of his power in us who believe, according to the working of his great might
which he accomplished in Christ when he raised him from the dead and made him sit at his right hand in the heavenly places,
far above all rule and authority and power and dominion, and above every name that is named, not only in this age but also in that which is to come;
and he has put all things under his feet and has made him the head over all things for the church,
which is his body, the fulness of him who fills all in all.
Read full chapter

exegesis.summary: St. Paul prays that the Ephesians may receive a spirit of wisdom to grasp the hope of their calling and the immeasurable power of God at work in believers. That power is the same power by which the Father raised Christ from the dead and seated him at his right hand, far above every rule and authority. The exalted Christ has been made head of the Church, his Body, the fullness of him who fills all in all.

patristicQuotes.quote: That he may reign with Christ, to whom the Father has committed all that is His. And as the son of a good father set over all that is his, He shall communicate of His dignity and glory to His faithful and wise stewards, that they also may be above the whole creation.

The people were astonished at the greatness of God, because of the gift of Christ, who conferred on the holy Apostles also the power of working divine miracles, and having the mastery over evil spirits.

cccReferences.title: The Church blesses the Father for inexpressible gifts
Christians as temple of the Spirit
Christ's glorification at center of New Testament

exegesis.thematicConnection: Paul gives the theological meaning of what Acts narrates and the Psalm celebrates: the ascended Christ is enthroned in heavenly glory and rules as Head of his Body, the Church. This is the 'hope of our calling' that empowers the missionary mandate Jesus gives in the Gospel.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text:
The Commissioning of the Disciples
Now the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them.
And when they saw him they worshiped him; but some doubted.
And Jesus came and said to them, “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.
Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit,
teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you; and lo, I am with you always, to the close of the age.”
Read full chapter

exegesis.summary: On a mountain in Galilee, the Risen Jesus appears to the Eleven, who worship him though some still doubt. He declares that all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to him and sends them to make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. He promises his abiding presence to the end of the age — the Great Commission with which Matthew's Gospel closes.

patristicQuotes.quote: When coming to His Passion the Lord had said to His disciples, 'After I am risen I will go before you into Galilee'; and the Angel said the same to the women. Therefore the disciples obey the command of their Master. Eleven only go, for one had already perished.

The disciples, when they saw Him, knew the Lord, and worshipped Him, bowing their faces to the ground. And He, their affectionate and merciful Master, that He might take away all doubtfulness from their hearts, coming to them, strengthened them in their belief, saying, All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth.
That the disciples may ever remember that they are the children of one parent, and that by their new birth they have passed the limits of their earthly origin.

cccReferences.title: The call resounds throughout the world

exegesis.thematicConnection: Matthew's commission is the hinge of the entire Solemnity: the Lord enthroned (Psalm), exalted as Head of the Church (Ephesians), and lifted into heaven (Acts) does not abandon his own. His Ascension is the inauguration of the Church's mission, sealed with his promise: 'I am with you always.'

synthesis.title: He ascends as King — and sends his Church
synthesis.body: On this Solemnity of the Ascension, the Church does not mourn a departure; she celebrates an enthronement. Psalm 47 supplies the soundtrack — 'God mounts his throne amid shouts of joy' — while Acts narrates the historical event: Jesus is lifted up before the apostles' eyes, taken from sight by a cloud, the same cloud that veiled God's glory on Sinai and at the Transfiguration. St. Paul, writing to the Ephesians, unveils the meaning: the Father has seated Christ at his right hand, far above every power, and has made him Head of the Church, his Body, 'the fullness of him who fills all in all.' This is no absence. It is presence in a new and greater mode. The Lord who ascends is the same Lord who, in Matthew's Gospel, gathers the Eleven on the mountain and declares, 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.' From that authority flows the mission of the Church: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations.' The Ascension and the Great Commission are one mystery — Christ goes to the Father precisely so that, through the Spirit he will pour out at Pentecost, he may be with his Church always, to the close of the age. We stand, then, not gazing into heaven, but sent.
